attribution.name: PlayfulProcess
attribution.note: Original grammar created from memory. A bilingual inventory of the violence hiding in everyday Portuguese and English — the sayings we repeat without thinking, the songs we sing to children before they can understand, the casual cruelty that colonialism, antisemitism, racism, and classism planted in our mouths. Each entry includes the original, its translation, its colonial root, and a decolonial reflection. Because decolonizing starts with the tongue.

name: Línguas Coloniais / Colonial Tongues: The Violence We Speak Without Knowing
description:
Every language carries its history in its idioms. In Portuguese: 'judiar' means to mistreat — from 'judeu' (Jew). 'Denegrir' means to defame — from 'negro' (black). A nightstand is a 'criado-mudo' — a mute servant. In English: 'gypped' comes from 'Gypsy.' 'Sold down the river' was literal slave trading. Children sing 'Eeny Meeny Miny Moe' not knowing it once used the n-word. This grammar maps the colonial, antisemitic, racist, classist, and religious violence embedded in everyday speech in both Portuguese and English, with mutual translations that reveal how the same structures of oppression speak through different mouths. Includes children's songs — because colonialism's deepest move is to get into the lullaby.

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Brazilian cordel literature woodcuts (xilogravura). 19th-century children's book illustrations (Kate Greenaway, Walter Crane). Portuguese colonial-era broadsheets. WPA poster art. Brazilian folk art prints.

items.name: Judiação / Judiar — The Antisemitism in 'Mistreatment'

sections.The Saying:
PORTUGUÊS: 'Que judiação!' / 'Para de judiar desse menino!' (Stop mistreating that boy!)

ENGLISH TRANSLATION: 'What cruelty!' / 'Stop mistreating that boy!'
sections.The Colonial Root: 'Judiar' comes directly from 'judeu' — Jew. The verb means to mistreat, to torment, to be cruel. It encodes the medieval Christian narrative that Jews are inherently cruel — torturers of Christ, poisoners of wells, abusers of children. Portugal expelled its Jewish population in 1497 (or forced conversion to 'cristãos-novos'), and the Inquisition hunted crypto-Jews for centuries. The expulsion ended. The language didn't. Every Brazilian who says 'judiação' without thinking is speaking the Inquisition's verdict into the air.
sections.The Violence: The violence is ontological: it makes a people synonymous with cruelty. A child hearing 'judiar' learns, before they ever meet a Jewish person, that 'Jew' = 'one who torments.' The word doesn't need the child to be antisemitic — it does the work automatically, embedding the equation in the grammar of everyday compassion. You say 'judiação' when you feel pity. The pity itself carries the poison.
sections.The Mirror: ENGLISH EQUIVALENT: 'Gypped' / 'Don't gyp me!' — from 'Gypsy' (Roma people). Same structure: an entire people reduced to a negative verb. To be 'gypped' is to be cheated, encoding the stereotype that Roma are thieves. Both words hide genocide in casual speech — the Holocaust killed 6 million Jews and up to 500,000 Roma. Both peoples survive. Both slurs persist.
sections.Decolonial Reflection: Replacing 'judiar' with 'maltratar' (mistreat) costs nothing and erases nothing valuable. The resistance to changing is itself revealing: 'But it's just an expression!' Yes — and that's exactly how colonialism works. It becomes 'just' the way things are. The casual is the structural. The habit is the history.

items.name: Denegrir — To Blacken Is to Defame

sections.The Saying:
PORTUGUÊS: 'Não vou denegrir a imagem dele.' (I won't tarnish his image.)

ENGLISH TRANSLATION: 'I won't denigrate/blacken his image.'
sections.The Colonial Root: 'Denegrir' comes from 'negro' (black) via Latin 'denigrare' — to make black. In both Portuguese and English, the word equates blackness with moral corruption, dishonor, and stain. This is not ancient metaphor — it is the linguistic infrastructure of anti-Black racism, refined through centuries of Portuguese slave trading (Portugal was the first European nation to engage in the transatlantic slave trade, beginning in the 1440s). To 'blacken' someone's reputation is to make them darker. The language assumes whiteness as the default of honor.
sections.The Violence: The violence is chromatic: it maps a moral hierarchy onto skin color. Black = bad, dirty, dangerous, dishonest. White = pure, clean, innocent, honorable. This binary runs through both Portuguese and English like a spinal cord: 'lista negra' (blacklist), 'mercado negro' (black market), 'ovelha negra' (black sheep), 'magia negra' (black magic). Every use reinforces the equation. A child who learns that 'black' means 'bad' in language will carry that equation into a world where Black people exist. The word does the racism before the person does.
sections.The Mirror: ENGLISH EQUIVALENT: 'Denigrate,' 'blacken,' 'blacklist,' 'black sheep,' 'black market,' 'black magic' — identical structure. Also: 'dark' as metaphor for ignorance or evil ('the Dark Ages,' 'dark times,' 'a dark chapter'). And the reverse: 'pure as snow,' 'white lie' (a harmless lie — even dishonesty is gentler when it's white), 'whitewash.'
sections.Decolonial Reflection: 'Difamar' (defame), 'manchar' (stain), 'prejudicar' (harm) all work without the chromatic violence. The resistance: 'But denegrir comes from Latin!' Yes. Latin was an empire too. Etymology is not absolution — it's evidence. The deeper the root, the older the violence.

items.name: A Coisa Tá Preta — When 'Things Are Black' Means 'Things Are Bad'

sections.The Saying:
PORTUGUÊS: 'A coisa tá preta.' (Things are looking bad.) / 'A situação ficou preta.' (The situation turned dark/bad.)

ENGLISH TRANSLATION: 'Things are looking black/dark.' / 'The situation went dark.'
sections.The Colonial Root: Brazil was the last country in the Americas to abolish slavery (1888 — the Lei Áurea) and received approximately 4.9 million enslaved Africans — more than any other nation. 'A coisa tá preta' is the linguistic residue of 400 years of slavery: when things get difficult, dangerous, or hopeless, they 'turn Black.' The expression maps the condition of enslaved people onto the concept of difficulty itself. Blackness = hardship. The language makes the enslaved person's skin the synonym for their suffering, as if the suffering were the color and not the chains.
sections.The Violence: When a white Brazilian says 'a coisa tá preta' in front of a Black Brazilian, both hear the equation. One can ignore it. The other cannot. The violence is in the asymmetry: the expression is invisible to those it doesn't describe and inescapable for those it does. It teaches Black children that their existence is a metaphor for difficulty. Their skin is an idiom for 'things going wrong.'
sections.The Mirror: ENGLISH EQUIVALENT: 'Things look dark,' 'a black day,' 'the situation is bleak' (from Old Norse 'bleikr,' meaning pale — so 'bleak' originally meant 'becoming white/pale,' but it evolved to mean barren). The English chromatic system is just as infected: darkness = danger, lightness = hope. 'Bringing something to light' = making it known. 'Being in the dark' = being ignorant.
sections.Decolonial Reflection: 'A coisa tá difícil' (things are difficult), 'a situação tá complicada' (things are complicated) — simple, accurate, non-violent. The phrase loses nothing except its racism. That's always the test: if removing the colonial content removes nothing useful, the colonial content was never the point. It was just the habit.

items.name: Serviço de Preto — 'Black Person's Work' = Shoddy Work

sections.The Saying:
PORTUGUÊS: 'Isso é serviço de preto.' (This is shoddy/bad work.) / 'Ficou um serviço de preto.' (It ended up being poor quality work.)

ENGLISH TRANSLATION: 'That's a hack job.' / 'That's sloppy work.'
sections.The Colonial Root: For 400 years, enslaved Black people in Brazil were forced to do all the physical labor — building, farming, cooking, cleaning, carrying, constructing — under conditions designed to produce failure: insufficient tools, exhaustion, malnutrition, violence. When the work wasn't perfect (because how could it be?), the imperfection was attributed not to the conditions but to the race. 'Serviço de preto' encodes the slave-owner's logic: the enslaved person's forced labor is inferior because the enslaved person is inferior. The chains are invisible; the blame is visible.
sections.The Violence: This expression attacks the one thing colonialism pretended to value in Black people: their labor. Having stolen their bodies for work, the colonial system then declared the work worthless. It's a double theft — steal the labor, then defame it. A Black professional who does excellent work in Brazil still exists in a linguistic environment where 'Black work' means 'bad work.' The expression is waiting in the language like a trap, ready to be activated every time someone is dissatisfied with a result.
sections.The Mirror: ENGLISH EQUIVALENT: No exact parallel, but the structure exists in: 'Jerry-rigged' (sometimes conflated with 'jury-rigged' — the 'Jerry' possibly referring to Germans/WWII), 'ghetto' as adjective meaning cheap/low-quality, 'Mickey Mouse operation' (trivializing). The closest structural parallel is the way 'welfare queen' encoded race into economic judgment — Black poverty as moral failure, never structural.
sections.Decolonial Reflection: 'Serviço mal feito' (poorly done work), 'trabalho malfeito' — the language has perfectly adequate non-racist alternatives. The question to ask someone who uses 'serviço de preto' is: who built this country? Who built the churches, the houses, the farms, the roads? Black hands built Brazil. The expression insults the builders while living in what they built.

items.name: Programa de Índio — When 'Indian Plans' Mean Boring Plans

sections.The Saying:
PORTUGUÊS: 'Isso é programa de índio.' (That's a boring/bad plan.) / 'Que programa de índio!' (What a lame activity!)

ENGLISH TRANSLATION: 'That's a lame plan.' / 'How boring!'
sections.The Colonial Root: The expression reduces the entire existence of Brazil's indigenous peoples — over 300 distinct nations, hundreds of languages, millennia of sophisticated ecological knowledge, complex social structures, astronomical observation, agricultural engineering — to 'boring.' The colonial logic: indigenous life has no entertainment value, no complexity, no interest. It is the blank space on the map. The expression inherits the colonial gaze that saw the Amazon as 'empty' — ignoring the people who had shaped it into the most biodiverse forest on Earth through thousands of years of intentional cultivation.
sections.The Violence: Brazil's indigenous population was approximately 3-5 million at European contact (1500). Today it is approximately 900,000. The genocide was physical (massacre, disease, displacement) and cultural (forced conversion, language death, land theft). 'Programa de índio' erases this history by reducing indigenous life to an adjective meaning 'boring.' The dead cannot object. The survivors hear their existence used as a synonym for worthlessness.
sections.The Mirror: ENGLISH EQUIVALENT: 'Indian giver' — someone who gives a gift and takes it back. The colonial inversion is breathtaking: Europeans took an entire continent from indigenous peoples and then created an expression accusing Native Americans of being the ones who don't honor their deals. Also: 'too many chiefs, not enough Indians' (hierarchy as white/indigenous), 'off the reservation' (someone out of control — the control was a concentration camp).
sections.Decolonial Reflection: 'Programa chato' (boring plan), 'programa sem graça' (uninteresting plan). But the deeper decolonial move is to ask: what actually constitutes a rich evening for a Yanomami family, a Guarani community, a Kayapó gathering? The answer — storytelling, star-watching, song, communal work, spiritual practice — reveals that the 'boring' plan might be richer than anything Netflix offers. The expression reveals more about the speaker's impoverishment than the indigenous person's.

items.name: Criado-Mudo — The Nightstand Is a 'Mute Servant'

sections.The Saying:
PORTUGUÊS: 'Põe o copo no criado-mudo.' (Put the glass on the nightstand.)

ENGLISH TRANSLATION: 'Put the glass on the nightstand.' (Literal: 'Put the glass on the mute servant.')
sections.The Colonial Root: The 'criado-mudo' — the small table beside the bed — is named for the enslaved person who stood silently beside the master's bed, holding a tray, a candle, a glass of water, throughout the night. The servant had to be 'mudo' — mute, silent, a piece of furniture that breathed. When the human was replaced by wood, the name stayed. Every Brazilian who says 'criado-mudo' is naming a piece of furniture after the enslaved person it replaced. The table inherited the slave's function and their silence.
sections.The Violence: The violence is the casual transformation of a human being into an object — and the fact that the object kept the human's name. The criado-mudo is the most literal colonial artifact in the Portuguese language: a word that documents the exact moment when a person became a thing. And the thing is so common, so everyday, that the documentation has become invisible. You sleep beside it every night. You never think about who stood there before the wood.
sections.The Mirror: ENGLISH EQUIVALENT: 'Master bedroom' — the room of the slave master. 'Master/slave' in computing (now being replaced with 'primary/replica' or 'main/secondary'). 'Servant's quarters.' The domestic architecture of colonial homes is encoded in English real estate language. But no English word quite matches the criado-mudo's brutal specificity — a piece of furniture named after the enslaved person it replaced, still in daily use, still in every home.
sections.Decolonial Reflection: 'Mesa de cabeceira' (bedside table) is the alternative — already widely used, especially in Portugal. The shift is happening, slowly. But the fact that 'criado-mudo' persists in Brazilian Portuguese reveals how deeply slavery is embedded in domestic life. The furniture has been liberated from the human. The language hasn't.

items.name: Mulata — From 'Mula' (Mule): The Word That Breeds You Out of Humanity

sections.The Saying:
PORTUGUÊS: 'Ela é uma mulata bonita.' (She's a beautiful mulata.) / 'A mulata brasileira.' (The Brazilian mulata.)

ENGLISH TRANSLATION: 'She's a beautiful mixed-race woman.' (Literal root: 'She's a beautiful mule.')
sections.The Colonial Root: 'Mulato/a' derives from 'mula' (mule) — the sterile offspring of a horse and a donkey. The word was created by colonial zoological logic: if a white person and a Black person produce offspring, the child is a hybrid, like a mule — mixed, unnatural, categorically unstable. The word animalizes mixed-race people by placing them in a taxonomy of breeding, not of humanity. In Brazil, 'mulata' has been sexualized and commodified into a cultural brand — Carnival 'mulatas,' the 'mulata exportação' (export-grade mulata), the 'mulata' as Brazil's erotic national symbol. The animal metaphor became a sexual commodity.
sections.The Violence: The violence operates on multiple levels: racial (you are an animal hybrid), sexual (your value is erotic), and ontological (you don't fully exist as a category — you are between categories, like a mule between species). A 'mulata bonita' is a compliment that dehumanizes: you are beautiful, like a well-bred animal. The word makes mixed-race women available for consumption — their bodies are public, their desirability is their identity, their humanity is negotiable.
sections.The Mirror: ENGLISH EQUIVALENT: 'Mulatto' (same root, same animal logic). Also: 'half-breed,' 'mixed' (as if purity were the default and mixture were the deviation), 'exotic' (when applied to mixed-race people — 'exotic' means 'not from here,' making the person permanently foreign in their own country). The entire vocabulary of racial mixing in both languages is a vocabulary of animal husbandry.
sections.Decolonial Reflection: 'Parda,' 'negra,' 'afrodescendente,' or simply the person's name. The deepest decolonial move is to refuse the racial taxonomy entirely — but in a society structured by race, naming race accurately (without animal metaphors) is a necessary first step. When someone says 'but mulata is a compliment!' ask: a compliment to whom? From whose gaze? The mule didn't choose its name either.

items.name: Vixi Maria / Virgem Maria — The Catholic Colony in Every Gasp

sections.The Saying:
PORTUGUÊS: 'Vixi Maria!' / 'Virgem Maria!' / 'Nossa Senhora!' / 'Ave Maria!' / 'Cruz credo!' / 'Se Deus quiser!' / 'Graças a Deus!'

ENGLISH TRANSLATION: 'Oh my Virgin Mary!' / 'Our Lady!' / 'Hail Mary!' / 'Cross and creed!' / 'God willing!' / 'Thank God!'
sections.The Colonial Root:
Portuguese colonization was inseparable from Catholic evangelization — the two arrived on the same ships. The Padroado (the agreement between the Portuguese crown and the Vatican) meant that colonizing was literally Christianizing: every territory claimed was a territory to be converted. Indigenous religions were 'paganism.' African religions brought by enslaved people were 'witchcraft.' The Inquisition operated in Brazil. Catholic vocabulary saturated the language so completely that it became invisible — you can't express surprise, relief, hope, or fear in Brazilian Portuguese without invoking Catholic theology. 'Vixi' is a contraction of 'Virgem' (Virgin). 'Cruz credo' was the Inquisition's creed formula. Your gasp is a prayer you didn't choose.

NOTE: Not all religious expressions are equally colonial. 'Se Deus quiser' (God willing) and 'Graças a Deus' (Thank God) express a universal human impulse — humility before the unknown, gratitude for survival — that exists across cultures and religions. 'Inshallah' (Arabic), 'Deo volente' (Latin), 'b'ezrat Hashem' (Hebrew) carry the same meaning. The impulse is human; the specific God invoked is where the colonial question begins. 'Se Deus quiser' may be genuinely neutral — an expression of faith that transcends any single religion's colonial history. The colonial charge is strongest in the specifically Catholic markers: 'Virgem Maria,' 'Nossa Senhora,' 'Cruz credo' — these are not universal prayers but brand identifiers of the particular church that arrived with the ships.
sections.The Violence: The violence is in the monopoly, not in the sacred. When every exclamation is Catholic, every moment of emotion is colonized — not because invoking the divine is wrong, but because only one divine is available in the language. An Afro-Brazilian practicing Candomblé says 'Vixi Maria' by reflex — not because they chose Mary, but because the language left no other option. 'Cruz credo' (Cross and creed) was literally the Inquisition's formula — the creed you were forced to recite to prove you weren't a heretic. Now it means 'yikes.' The question is not whether God belongs in language (God may well belong everywhere) but whether only one God's name gets to fill every exclamation.
sections.The Mirror: ENGLISH EQUIVALENT: 'Oh my God!' / 'Jesus Christ!' / 'God willing' / 'Bless you' (when sneezing) / 'For God's sake' / 'God forbid' / 'Goodbye' (contraction of 'God be with ye'). English is equally saturated with Christian exclamation, but with less Marian devotion (no Virgin Mary gasps in English — the Reformation stripped her out). Interestingly, 'God willing' in English is sometimes heard as almost Islamic ('Inshallah') by Americans unfamiliar with their own language's identical construction. The same humility before the divine sounds colonial in one mouth and foreign in another.
sections.Decolonial Reflection: This is the most nuanced entry in the grammar because faith is not colonialism — but the forced monopoly of one faith's vocabulary is. The decolonial move here is not to strip God from language but to notice which God the language defaults to, and to ask: was this default chosen or imposed? 'Se Deus quiser' may be genuinely sacred. 'Cruz credo' is the Inquisition in miniature. The tongue can hold both truths. Some practitioners of Candomblé and Umbanda have consciously expanded their exclamatory vocabulary with Yoruba-origin expressions ('Axé!' 'Eparrei!') — not replacing the divine but diversifying which divine gets to be spontaneous.

items.name: Sold Down the River — The Slave Trade in Your Vocabulary

sections.The Saying:
ENGLISH: 'He was sold down the river by his own partners.'

PORTUGUÊS: 'Ele foi traído pelos próprios sócios.' (He was betrayed by his own partners.)
sections.The Colonial Root: To be 'sold down the river' literally meant to be sold from the upper South (Virginia, Kentucky) to the cotton and sugar plantations of the Deep South (Mississippi, Louisiana) via the Mississippi River. This was the most feared fate for enslaved people — the Deep South's plantations were death camps with higher mortality rates, more brutal conditions, and further from any possibility of escape or family contact. To be 'sold down the river' was not a metaphor. It was a specific, documented, routine horror of American slavery.
sections.The Violence: The expression transforms one of slavery's most concrete horrors into a casual idiom for 'betrayal.' The violence is the trivialization: when a white office worker says 'my boss sold me down the river,' they equate a workplace disappointment with the destruction of families, the transport of human beings as cargo, and the death sentences of the cotton fields. The metaphor requires forgetting what it literally describes. That forgetting is the colonial work — transforming history into figure of speech, atrocity into idiom.
sections.The Mirror: PORTUGUÊS EQUIVALENT: No exact parallel, but 'dar uma de João sem braço' (play dumb, shirk responsibility) has some structural similarity. More directly: Brazilian Portuguese has its own slave-trade language hiding in plain sight — 'tronco' (trunk/post, the whipping post) persists in 'tronco' as a word for someone stubborn or stuck. 'Senzala' (slave quarters) is still used architecturally.
sections.Decolonial Reflection: 'Betrayed,' 'stabbed in the back,' 'double-crossed' — English has dozens of betrayal metaphors that don't require trivializing slavery. The resistance to change reveals the depth of the embedding: 'But no one thinks about slavery when they say it!' Exactly. That's the achievement of colonial language — it made the atrocity so common that it became invisible.

items.name: Eeny Meeny Miny Moe — The N-Word in the Nursery

sections.The Saying:
ENGLISH: 'Eeny, meeny, miny, moe / Catch a tiger by the toe / If he hollers, let him go / Eeny, meeny, miny, moe.'

PORTUGUÊS: 'Uni, duni, tê / Salamê, minguê / O sorvete colorê / Uni, duni, tê.' (The Brazilian counting rhyme — different content, same function.)
sections.The Colonial Root: The version every American child now learns says 'catch a tiger by the toe.' The version that existed from the mid-19th century through the mid-20th century said 'catch a n***** by the toe.' 'If he hollers, let him go' — the 'hollering' was not a tiger's roar but an enslaved person's scream. The rhyme is a counting game about catching a runaway slave. The 'tiger' substitution happened gradually in the mid-20th century, but millions of Americans alive today learned the original version in their childhood. The sanitization is recent; the wound is deep.
sections.The Violence: Children learn this rhyme before they understand its history — that's the point. The colonial move is to get into the body before the mind can object. A five-year-old who sings 'eeny meeny' while choosing who's 'it' in a playground game is performing a slave-catching ritual stripped of its content. The content is gone, but the rhythm remains in the body. The muscle memory of empire.
sections.The Mirror: PORTUGUÊS EQUIVALENT: The Brazilian 'Uni duni tê' is sanitized in content (ice cream, colors) but the mechanism is the same — counting rhymes that choose who's 'it,' who's included, who's excluded. The Portuguese tradition has its own violent counting rhymes: 'Lá em cima do piano / Tem um copo de veneno / Quem bebeu morreu / O azar foi seu' (Up on the piano / There's a glass of poison / Whoever drank it died / Bad luck for you). The violence migrated from racial to general, but the structure of casual cruelty in children's games persists.
sections.Decolonial Reflection: The 'tiger' version is already the decolonial replacement — imperfect, because the rhythm still carries the shape of the original. Some educators argue for dropping the rhyme entirely. Others say: teach the history alongside the rhyme. Let children know what they're singing over. The erasure of the n-word version is itself a kind of violence — it lets white America pretend the original never existed. The decolonial move is not sanitization but honesty.

items.name: Grandfathered In — The Voting Booth as Colonial Architecture

sections.The Saying:
ENGLISH: 'That policy was grandfathered in.' / 'You're grandfathered into the old pricing.'

PORTUGUÊS: 'Essa regra foi mantida por direito adquirido.' (That rule was maintained as an acquired right.)
sections.The Colonial Root: 'Grandfather clause' was a legal mechanism used in the post-Reconstruction American South (1890s-1910s) to prevent Black people from voting while allowing illiterate and poor white people to vote. The laws required voters to pass literacy tests and pay poll taxes — but exempted anyone whose grandfather had been eligible to vote before the Civil War. Since no enslaved person's grandfather could have voted, the clause was a perfect racial filter disguised as a neutral, family-based exemption. It was Jim Crow's most elegant tool: racist without ever mentioning race.
sections.The Violence: When a tech company says 'existing users are grandfathered into the old plan,' they're using a word invented to disenfranchise Black voters. The violence is not in the current usage (which is genuinely race-neutral in intent) but in the laundering: a tool of racial oppression has been cleaned, pressed, and returned to public use as a friendly business term. The word's racist origin has been 'grandfathered' out of its own history.
sections.The Mirror: PORTUGUÊS EQUIVALENT: 'Direito adquirido' (acquired right) is the closest functional parallel — and it's clean. Portuguese didn't need a 'grandfather' metaphor because Brazilian voter suppression worked through different mechanisms (literacy requirements persisted until 1985, effectively disenfranchising millions of poor, predominantly Black Brazilians). Same outcome, different vocabulary. The colonial architecture is in the policy, not always in the word.
sections.Decolonial Reflection: 'Legacy,' 'pre-existing,' 'exempt under the previous terms' — all work without invoking voter suppression. The word persists because its origin has been forgotten. Forgetting is the colonial lingua franca.

items.name: Peanut Gallery — The Cheap Seats and the Bodies That Filled Them

sections.The Saying:
ENGLISH: 'No comments from the peanut gallery!' (Stop heckling / No unsolicited opinions!)

PORTUGUÊS: 'A galera do fundão que se cale!' (The back-row crowd should shut up!)
sections.The Colonial Root: The 'peanut gallery' was the uppermost balcony in vaudeville and minstrel-show theaters — the cheapest seats, typically the only ones available to Black audience members under segregation. The name came from the peanuts sold as cheap snacks in these sections (and sometimes thrown at performers). To dismiss someone's opinion as coming from 'the peanut gallery' is to invoke the segregated theater's architecture: the people up there — the Black people, the poor people — their opinions don't count. Heckling from the peanut gallery was the only form of critique available to those who couldn't sit where power sat.
sections.The Violence: The expression silences by invoking spatial hierarchy: your opinion comes from the wrong seat, the cheap seat, the Black seat. It's a dismissal that carries the entire architecture of segregation in six syllables. When a boss says 'no comments from the peanut gallery,' they may not know the history — but the structure knows. The high seats dismiss the low seats. The logic of apartheid survives as office humor.
sections.The Mirror: PORTUGUÊS EQUIVALENT: 'Galinheiro' (chicken coop) — the cheap upper balcony in Brazilian theaters. Same spatial hierarchy (high = cheap = worthless), different animal metaphor. Also: 'geral' (general admission, standing room) in futebol stadiums — historically the space of the poor, now increasingly eliminated by all-seated 'arena' stadiums. The erasure of cheap seats is the completion of the colonial move: first you mock the people in them, then you remove the seats entirely.
sections.Decolonial Reflection: The heckling from the peanut gallery was democracy — the only form of participation available to the excluded. When we silence 'the peanut gallery,' we reproduce the theater's hierarchy in any room we occupy. The decolonial ear hears the heckle not as noise but as the voice that the architecture tried to marginalize.

items.name: Uppity — The Word That Polices Rising

sections.The Saying:
ENGLISH: 'She's getting a bit uppity.' / 'Don't get uppity with me.'

PORTUGUÊS: 'Ela tá se achando.' / 'Não se acha não.' (She's getting full of herself. / Don't get above yourself.)
sections.The Colonial Root: 'Uppity' emerged in the post-Reconstruction American South as a specifically racial adjective: an 'uppity Negro' was a Black person who didn't 'know their place' — who acted as if they deserved the same rights, spaces, or respect as white people. The word was a warning shot: you are rising above your station, and rising has consequences. Emmett Till was murdered at 14 for being 'uppity' — for allegedly whistling at a white woman. The word is an execution order disguised as a social observation.
sections.The Violence: The violence is in the direction: 'uppity' only moves one way — from the powerful to the less powerful. No one calls a billionaire 'uppity.' The word exists specifically to police upward movement by people who are supposed to stay down. In the American context, it is inseparable from lynching — the physical enforcement of the hierarchy that 'uppity' named. Today it's used against women, Black people, immigrants, and anyone who claims space that power considers its own.
sections.The Mirror: PORTUGUÊS EQUIVALENT: 'Se achando' (thinking you're something), 'metido/a' (presumptuous), 'quer ser mais do que é' (wants to be more than they are). Brazilian Portuguese has the same structure: words that punish ambition in people who 'shouldn't' have it. 'Ele não sabe o lugar dele' (He doesn't know his place) — direct parallel. The racial coding in Brazil is subtler (because racism is denied more comprehensively) but the mechanism is identical: language that keeps the lower castes down.
sections.Decolonial Reflection: The decolonial response to 'uppity' is to ask: up relative to what? Who defined 'their place'? The word reveals the speaker's need for hierarchy more than it reveals anything about the person being described. When someone is called 'uppity,' the speaker is confessing that they need someone below them to feel comfortable. The confession is the diagnosis.

items.name: Thug — From Hindi Temple to Black Criminalization

sections.The Saying:
ENGLISH: 'Those thugs need to be locked up.' / 'Thug life.'

PORTUGUÊS: 'Esses bandidos precisam ser presos.' / 'Marginal.' / 'Vagabundo.'
sections.The Colonial Root: 'Thug' comes from the Hindi 'thag' (ठग) — a member of organized bands of robbers in colonial India who were said to strangle travelers as devotees of the goddess Kali. The British colonial administration sensationalized and likely exaggerated the 'Thuggee' phenomenon to justify the Thuggee and Dacoity Suppression Act (1836) — one of the tools of British control over Indian populations. The word traveled from Hindi to English as a colonial trophy: proof that India was dangerous and needed British 'civilization.' In 20th-century America, 'thug' was transferred from Indians to Black people, becoming the media's preferred euphemism for the n-word — 'thug' meant 'Black criminal' without requiring anyone to say the quiet part loud.
sections.The Violence: When American media calls a Black teenager a 'thug,' two colonial histories overlap: British colonialism dehumanizing Indians and American racism criminalizing Black bodies. The word carries both empires' weight. Richard Sherman, the Stanford-educated NFL player, said it plainly: 'The reason it bothers me is because it seems like it's an accepted way of calling somebody the n-word.' The word is a laundry service for racism — it comes out clean enough for the news.
sections.The Mirror: PORTUGUÊS EQUIVALENT: 'Marginal,' 'vagabundo,' 'bandido,' 'elemento' — all racially coded in Brazilian Portuguese. When Brazilian news says 'os marginais foram abatidos' (the criminals were shot down), the audience knows the skin color without being told. 'Elemento suspeito' (suspicious element) — the word 'element' reduces a person to a chemical compound, something to be isolated and neutralized. Same colonial logic, different vocabulary.
sections.Decolonial Reflection: 'Suspect,' 'perpetrator,' 'person accused of' — specific, legal, non-racial language exists. The choice to use 'thug' instead is itself the tell. When language has a precise legal alternative and the speaker chooses the colonial word instead, the choice is the confession.

items.name: A Barata Diz Que Tem — The Cockroach Who Lies About Being Rich

sections.The Saying:
PORTUGUÊS: 'A Barata diz que tem / Sete saias de filó / É mentira da Barata / Ela tem é uma só / Ah ah ah / Oh oh oh / Ela tem é uma só!'

(The Cockroach says she has / Seven tulle skirts / It's a lie from the Cockroach / She only has one / Ha ha ha / Ho ho ho / She only has one!)

'A Barata diz que tem / Um sapato de veludo / É mentira da Barata / O pé dela é peludo!'

(The Cockroach says she has / A velvet shoe / It's a lie from the Cockroach / Her foot is hairy!)
sections.The Colonial Root: This children's song teaches class surveillance from the cradle. The 'Barata' (cockroach) is a poor creature who pretends to have the markers of wealth — silk skirts, velvet shoes, a diamond ring — and is publicly humiliated for the pretension. The song is a communal shaming ritual: 'Ha ha ha! She only has one!' The children laugh together at someone who tried to rise above their station. The cockroach is the 'uppity' poor person, the servant who wears the mistress's castoffs, the favela child in a knock-off brand. The song teaches: know your place, and if you try to leave it, we will laugh at you.
sections.The Violence: The violence is in the collective laughter. 'Ha ha ha! Oh oh oh!' — the song doesn't just identify the lie, it celebrates the exposure. Children learn that pretending to have more than you are is the worst crime, worse than having nothing. The cockroach — already the lowest creature, the one you step on — is punished for aspiration. In a country built on the myth of 'racial democracy' that actually runs on brutal class hierarchy, this song is the curriculum: don't rise. Don't pretend. Don't imagine yourself in silk when you belong in rags. And if you do — we will all laugh together.
sections.The Mirror: ENGLISH EQUIVALENT: 'Baa, Baa, Black Sheep' — ostensibly about wool taxation (one third for the master, one third for the dame, none for the little boy down the lane). The class structure is less hidden: someone works, someone else takes. Also: the entire genre of nursery rhymes about poverty — 'Old Mother Hubbard' (the cupboard was bare), 'Jack Sprat' (who could eat no fat) — poverty as entertainment, deprivation as comedy.
sections.Decolonial Reflection: What if the cockroach's 'lie' is a dream? What if she imagines herself in tulle because imagination is the only luxury available to the poor? The decolonial re-reading of 'A Barata' transforms the song from mockery into elegy: a creature at the bottom of the hierarchy dares to imagine beauty, and the world punishes her for it. The laughter is the violence. The dream is the resistance.

items.name: Atirei o Pau no Gato — I Threw a Stick at the Cat

sections.The Saying:
PORTUGUÊS: 'Atirei o pau no gato-to / Mas o gato-to / Não morreu-reu-reu / Dona Chica-ca / Admirou-se-se / Do berro, do berro que o gato deu: / Miau!'

(I threw a stick at the cat / But the cat / Didn't die / Dona Chica / Was amazed / By the scream, the scream the cat gave: / Meow!)
sections.The Colonial Root: A children's song about attempted animal murder, sung cheerfully. The narrator throws a stick at a cat — an act of casual violence — and the punchline is not remorse but surprise that the cat survived. 'Dona Chica' (a wealthy woman — 'Dona' is the title of a lady of the house) is 'amazed' not by the cruelty but by the noise. The entire moral architecture is colonial-patriarchal: violence is entertainment, survival is comedy, and the woman of the house is a spectator to cruelty she doesn't interrupt.
sections.The Violence: This song normalizes: (1) violence against the vulnerable (the cat), (2) indifference to suffering (Dona Chica is amazed, not horrified), (3) the expectation of death as the natural outcome of violence (the surprise is that the cat didn't die). Children sing this while clapping hands, bouncing on laps, laughing. The rhythm makes the violence musical. The repetition ('gato-to, morreu-reu-reu') turns cruelty into a tongue-twister game. By the time a child understands what the words mean, the song already lives in their body as joy.
sections.The Mirror: ENGLISH EQUIVALENT: 'Three Blind Mice' — 'She cut off their tails with a carving knife / Did you ever see such a sight in your life?' Same structure: animal mutilation as nursery entertainment. Also: 'Rock-a-bye Baby' — 'When the bough breaks, the cradle will fall / And down will come baby, cradle and all.' A lullaby about a baby falling from a tree. We sing our children to sleep with the image of their own destruction. The colonial tongue starts in the cradle.
sections.Decolonial Reflection: Some Brazilian educators have created alternative versions: 'Não atire o pau no gato / Porque isso não se faz / O gatinho é nosso amigo / Não devemos maltratar' (Don't throw a stick at the cat / Because that's wrong / The kitten is our friend / We shouldn't mistreat it). The revision is well-intentioned but loses the decolonial opportunity: rather than sanitize, teach children to hear the violence in what they sing. The awareness is the education.

items.name: Escravos de Jó — Slaves of Job (The Slavery Game)

sections.The Saying:
PORTUGUÊS: 'Escravos de Jó / Jogavam caxangá / Tira, põe / Deixa ficar / Guerreiros com guerreiros / Fazem zigue-zigue-zá / Guerreiros com guerreiros / Fazem zigue-zigue-zá.'

(Slaves of Job / Played caxangá / Take out, put in / Leave it be / Warriors with warriors / Make zig-zag-za / Warriors with warriors / Make zig-zag-za.)
sections.The Colonial Root: Children sit in a circle passing objects (stones, cups) to the rhythm of a song that literally begins with the word 'Escravos' — slaves. 'Caxangá' may derive from a Bantu word for a type of game played by enslaved Africans. The song preserves the memory of enslaved people's recreation — the games they played in the senzala (slave quarters) — but strips it of context, transforms it into entertainment, and puts it in the mouths of children who don't know they're reenacting the leisure of the enslaved. The 'guerreiros' (warriors) may reference the Palmares quilombo or other resistance movements, now reduced to a hand-clapping pattern.
sections.The Violence: The violence is in the domestication. A slave song — perhaps a survival song, a resistance song, a code — has been absorbed into the children's canon as a motor-skills game. The word 'escravos' is sung by children who have never been taught what it means. Slavery becomes a rhythm. The enslaved person becomes a game piece ('tira, põe, deixa ficar' — take out, put in, leave it). The song teaches children to handle slavery as casually as they handle the cups they're passing. That casualness is the education.
sections.The Mirror: ENGLISH EQUIVALENT: 'London Bridge Is Falling Down' — about destruction, possibly imprisonment ('Take the key and lock her up'). Also: 'Ring Around the Rosie' — widely (though debatably) linked to the plague ('Ashes, ashes, we all fall down' = death). Both English rhymes encode historical catastrophe as children's games. But neither begins with the word 'Slaves.' 'Escravos de Jó' is exceptional in its directness: it names the violence in its first word and then erases it with rhythm.
sections.Decolonial Reflection: Some educators argue the song should be kept — with teaching. The word 'escravos' in a children's song is an opportunity: the moment the child asks 'what does escravos mean?' is the moment the history can enter. The song is not the problem. The silence around it is. Singing 'Escravos de Jó' without teaching about slavery is the colonial move. Singing it and then teaching is the decolonial one.

items.name: Boi da Cara Preta — The Black-Faced Bull (Scaring Children with Blackness)

sections.The Saying:
PORTUGUÊS: 'Boi, boi, boi / Boi da cara preta / Pega essa menina / Que tem medo de careta.'

(Bull, bull, bull / Black-faced bull / Grab that girl / Who's afraid of scary faces.)
sections.The Colonial Root: A lullaby that threatens children with a black-faced creature that will 'pegar' (grab/catch) them if they don't behave. The 'cara preta' (black face) is the source of fear. The song belongs to the same family as 'bicho-papão' (bogeyman) threats, but with the specific chromatic coding of Brazilian racism: the scary thing is black. The threatening thing has a black face. Behave, or the black face will come for you. The lullaby teaches fear of blackness before the child can walk.
sections.The Violence: In a country where 56% of the population identifies as Black or pardo, a lullaby that uses blackness as a threat is a foundational lesson in racial hierarchy. The Black-faced bull is the bogeyman — the thing in the dark, the punishment for disobedience. Black face = danger. Black face = monster. A Black child singing this song is being taught to fear their own reflection. A white child singing it is being taught that Black faces are the thing you scare children with.
sections.The Mirror: ENGLISH EQUIVALENT: 'Hush, Little Baby' is gentler but still coercive (be quiet and you'll get rewards). The scarier parallel is the general tradition of the 'boogeyman' — and the way 'dark' and 'black' function as fear-words in English children's stories: 'the dark forest,' 'the black knight,' 'the shadow.' But no common English lullaby uses 'black face' as its specific threat with the directness of 'Boi da Cara Preta.'
sections.Decolonial Reflection: The decolonial question is not 'should we stop singing it?' but 'what does it mean that we sing it?' A culture that lulls its children to sleep with the threat of a black face has built its comfort on anti-Blackness at the most intimate level — the bedside, the lullaby, the mother's voice. The decolonial lullaby would need to undo fear at its root: making blackness beautiful before the child can be taught to fear it.

items.name: Sambalelê — Broken Head and Deserved Beating

sections.The Saying:
PORTUGUÊS: 'Sambalelê tá doente / Tá com a cabeça quebrada / Sambalelê precisava / De umas dezoito lambadas / Samba, samba, Sambalelê / Pisa na barra da saia, Sambalelê!'

(Sambalelê is sick / Has a broken head / Sambalelê needed / About eighteen whippings / Samba, samba, Sambalelê / Step on the hem of the skirt, Sambalelê!)
sections.The Colonial Root: A children's song prescribing physical violence as medicine: Sambalelê is sick (broken head) and the cure is 'dezoito lambadas' — eighteen lashes. 'Lambada' in this context means a beating, a whipping. The name 'Sambalelê' has African-Brazilian roots (possibly from samba or Bantu origins). The song normalizes: (1) violence as discipline, (2) beating as cure, (3) the specific word 'lambada' (lash/whip) — the instrument of plantation punishment — applied cheerfully to a character with a name that codes as Black/poor. The song is the plantation logic set to a samba beat.
sections.The Violence: Children clap, dance, and sing about a person who needs to be beaten eighteen times. The rhythm makes the violence playful. The number (eighteen) makes it specific — not a vague threat but a precise prescription. 'Lambada' carries the weight of the slave-master's whip, now repurposed as a children's medicine. The same word ('lambada') that described the punishment of enslaved people describes the 'cure' for Sambalelê. The whip crosses from the senzala to the schoolyard without changing its name.
sections.The Mirror: ENGLISH EQUIVALENT: 'There Was an Old Woman Who Lived in a Shoe' — 'She whipped them all soundly and put them to bed.' Same structure: an overwhelmed authority figure who resolves the situation through mass beating. Also: 'Georgie Porgie, pudding and pie / Kissed the girls and made them cry / When the boys came out to play / Georgie Porgie ran away' — sexual harassment as nursery rhyme, with no consequence.
sections.Decolonial Reflection: The samba rhythm that makes this song irresistible is itself African-Brazilian: the body of the enslaved, their music, their rhythm, is being used to normalize their punishment. The song is a closed loop of colonial violence: African rhythm singing about whipping an African-coded character. The decolonial ear hears the samba and asks: whose body is dancing, and whose body is being beaten?

items.name: Rock-a-Bye Baby — The Lullaby That Drops the Child

sections.The Saying:
ENGLISH: 'Rock-a-bye baby, on the treetop / When the wind blows, the cradle will rock / When the bough breaks, the cradle will fall / And down will come baby, cradle and all.'

PORTUGUÊS: 'Nana, nenê / Que a cuca vai pegar / Papai foi pra roça / Mamãe foi trabalhar.' (Sleep, baby / The cuca monster will get you / Daddy went to the farm / Mommy went to work.)
sections.The Colonial Root: The English-speaking world's most famous lullaby describes an infant in a treetop cradle that falls when the branch breaks. The child plummets. That's the song. We sing this to babies to make them sleep. The Brazilian 'Nana Nenê' threatens with the 'cuca' (a monster from indigenous/African folklore, colonized into a bogeyman), then explains that both parents are absent — working. The child is alone with the monster. Both lullabies share the same structure: your protectors are gone, danger is real, sleep is your only escape.
sections.The Violence: Lullabies are the first poetry a human hears. They establish the relationship between safety (the parent's voice, the rocking motion) and content (abandonment, falling, monsters). The child's body learns: comfort and threat arrive together. Love and violence share a melody. This is the deepest colonial programming — not political, not racial, but existential: the world is dangerous, your parents cannot save you, sleep is surrender. Both 'Rock-a-Bye Baby' and 'Nana Nenê' prepare children for a world where authority comforts and terrifies simultaneously.
sections.The Mirror: Both songs function as training for obedience through fear. The English version uses nature (the wind, the branch) as the threat — an indifferent universe. The Portuguese version uses a monster and parental absence — a world where the adults who should protect you have been taken away by labor ('papai foi pra roça' — daddy went to the plantation/field). The Brazilian version is more explicitly colonial: the parents are not absent by choice but by economic necessity. The lullaby is a labor arrangement.
sections.Decolonial Reflection: What would a decolonial lullaby sound like? Perhaps: you are safe. I am here. The world is strange but you are held. Some indigenous lullaby traditions do exactly this — songs that welcome the child into a world of belonging, not danger. The colonial lullaby prepares the child for a hostile world. The decolonial lullaby insists: the hostility is not inevitable.

items.name: The Antisemitic Tongue — When Cruelty Speaks Yiddish

sections.About: 'Judiar' in Portuguese, 'gypped' in English — both languages turned persecuted peoples into verbs for bad behavior. The antisemitic tongue doesn't require the speaker to be antisemitic. It only requires them to speak the language without questioning it. The verb does the work. The speaker provides the breath. The Inquisition ended; the conjugation didn't.
sections.The Pattern: Both Portuguese and English encode the same antisemitic and anti-Roma logic: a people becomes a verb, and the verb means cruelty or dishonesty. The conversion from noun to verb is the colonial move — it transforms an identity into a behavior, making the behavior seem natural to the identity. You don't need to hate Jews to say 'judiação.' You just need to speak Portuguese.
sections.How to Use: Read 'Judiação' alongside 'Gypped.' Notice: same structure, different targets, different languages, same violence. Then ask: what other peoples have been turned into verbs in your language? What does the conjugation reveal about the culture's hierarchy of contempt?

items.name: The Anti-Black Tongue — When Darkness Means Danger

sections.About: The largest group in this grammar — because anti-Blackness is the largest colonial project in both Portuguese and English. From 'denegrir' to 'sold down the river,' from 'Boi da Cara Preta' to 'Eeny Meeny,' both languages are saturated with the equation: Black = bad, dark = dangerous, white = pure, light = good. The chromatic code runs so deep that it shapes not just racial language but all moral language. You can't describe evil in either language without invoking blackness. The slave trade ended. The vocabulary it left behind is still working.
sections.The Pattern: Three modes of anti-Black language: (1) CHROMATIC — black/dark as metaphor for evil, ignorance, danger (denegrir, blacklist, dark ages). (2) DIRECT — explicit racial coding in everyday speech (serviço de preto, uppity, thug). (3) MUSICAL — anti-Blackness embedded in rhythm and song (Boi da Cara Preta, Eeny Meeny, Sambalelê). The third mode is the most insidious because it enters through the body before the mind can object.
sections.How to Use: Read all anti-Black entries in sequence, alternating Portuguese and English. Notice: the two languages are accomplices. They share the same chromatic code, the same moral geography (dark = down = bad, light = up = good), the same history of enslavement. The Atlantic slave trade connected these two linguistic empires, and the vocabulary reflects the connection.

items.name: The Nursery — Where Colonialism Learns to Walk

sections.About: The most dangerous colonial language is the one that enters through the lullaby. Children's songs bypass the critical mind entirely — they go straight into the body through rhythm, repetition, and the warmth of the singing voice. By the time a child can understand the words of 'Escravos de Jó' or 'Eeny Meeny Miny Moe,' the song already lives in their muscle memory as joy. The colonial move is complete before the child can object: slavery is a game, violence is a melody, class hierarchy is a punchline, and blackness is the monster under the bed.
sections.The Pattern: Children's songs in both languages share a structure: (1) NORMALIZE VIOLENCE — throwing sticks at cats, whipping Sambalelê, catching slaves/tigers by the toe. (2) ENFORCE HIERARCHY — the cockroach who dares to imagine wealth is publicly humiliated. (3) TEACH FEAR — the black-faced bull, the cuca, the falling baby. (4) ERASE HISTORY — 'Escravos de Jó' is slavery as a hand-clapping game; 'Eeny Meeny' is slave-catching as counting. The rhythm makes the content invisible. That's the pedagogy.
sections.How to Use: Sing the songs out loud. Then read the analysis. Feel the dissonance: the body wants to clap, the mind recoils. That dissonance is the decolonial moment — the instant when the rhythm and the meaning separate, and you can see what was hiding in the melody. The education is not in banning the songs but in hearing them fully for the first time.

items.name: The Sacred Tongue — When Faith Becomes Monopoly

sections.About: This is the grammar's most nuanced group, because faith is not colonialism — but the enforced monopoly of one faith's vocabulary is. 'Se Deus quiser' (God willing) may be genuinely sacred, expressing a universal human humility before the unknown that appears in Arabic ('Inshallah'), Hebrew ('b'ezrat Hashem'), and a dozen other traditions. The colonial charge is not in invoking the divine but in which divine gets to be the default. 'Vixi Maria' and 'Cruz credo' are not universal prayers — they are brand identifiers of the specific church that arrived on the colonizer's ships. The decolonial ear learns to distinguish between the sacred impulse (human, universal, beautiful) and the sacred monopoly (colonial, imposed, suffocating).
sections.The Pattern: Both Portuguese and English are saturated with Christian exclamation, but differently. Portuguese carries specifically Catholic markers: the Virgin Mary, the saints, the cross, the creed. English carries Protestant markers: 'God' without intermediaries, 'Lord,' 'Heaven,' 'Hell.' Both colonized their linguistic worlds. But expressions like 'God willing' and 'Thank God' cross the colonial divide — they may be the rare case where the colonial vehicle carries genuinely universal cargo.
sections.How to Use: Listen to your own exclamations for a day. Count the religious references. Then ask: which ones feel like genuine faith, and which feel like a language that was installed without your consent? The distinction matters. Decolonizing does not mean secularizing — it means diversifying which sacred gets to be spontaneous.

items.name: The Portuguese Colonial Tongue — 500 Years in Every Syllable

sections.About: Brazilian Portuguese is perhaps the world's most colonial language disguised as the world's most beautiful one. It carries the Portuguese empire (1415-1999), the Atlantic slave trade (the largest forced migration in human history), the extermination of indigenous peoples (from millions to hundreds of thousands), the Inquisition, and Catholic evangelization — all in its daily idioms, its furniture names, its children's songs, its exclamations. Brazil is the country that says 'we don't have racism' in a language where 'to blacken' means 'to defame' and the nightstand is named after a slave.
sections.The Pattern: Portuguese colonial language operates through three mechanisms: (1) CHROMATIC CODING — black = bad across all contexts. (2) EUPHEMISTIC MEMORY — slavery survives in furniture names, children's games, and expressions so common they've become invisible. (3) RELIGIOUS SATURATION — Catholic vocabulary monopolizes the space of spontaneous emotion. The language is the colony's most successful export — it crossed the Atlantic, absorbed African and indigenous words (açaí, mandioca, samba, axé), and disguised the absorption as proof of cultural harmony ('racial democracy'). The absorption is real. The harmony is a lie the language tells beautifully.
sections.The Invitation: Speak Portuguese for one day with the decolonial ear turned on. Count the colonial expressions. Notice how many times you say something that carries a history you didn't choose. Then ask: what would it take to speak this language consciously — not to purify it (purity is itself a colonial obsession) but to hear it fully, with all its beauty and all its violence, at once?

items.name: The English Colonial Tongue — The Empire's Favorite Language

sections.About: English is the world's dominant language because it was the language of the world's dominant empires — British and American. It carries the slave trade ('sold down the river'), Jim Crow ('grandfathered in,' 'uppity,' 'peanut gallery'), British colonial India ('thug,' 'loot,' 'jungle,' 'veranda'), settler colonialism ('Indian giver,' 'off the reservation'), and the ongoing project of American global hegemony (English as the 'default' language of business, science, and the internet). The language's global dominance is itself the colonial achievement — you're reading this grammar in English because empire made it so.
sections.The Pattern: English colonial language operates through: (1) HISTORICAL LAUNDERING — slave-trade expressions cleaned into business idioms ('sold down the river,' 'grandfathered in'). (2) RACIAL EUPHEMISM — words that encode race without naming it ('thug,' 'uppity,' 'urban,' 'inner-city'). (3) GLOBAL DEFAULT — English as the 'neutral' language of international communication, making every other language 'foreign.' The most colonial thing about English is not any single expression but the assumption that English needs no translation.
sections.The Invitation: English speakers have a unique decolonial challenge: the language itself is the empire. You cannot step outside it without stepping outside global communication. The decolonial move is not to abandon English but to speak it as a second language — even if it's your first. To hear it as one language among many, not as the default. To notice what it assumes, what it erases, and what it cannot say.

items.name: The Bilingual Mirror — Two Tongues, One Colony

sections.About: Portuguese and English are not the same language. But they share the same colonial grammar. Both equate darkness with evil. Both turn persecuted peoples into verbs. Both hide slavery in everyday expressions. Both sing violence to children. Both saturate emotional vocabulary with Christian theology. The bilingual mirror reveals that colonialism is not a language but a structure — it speaks through any mouth, in any tongue, wherever power has organized the world into center and periphery, white and dark, master and servant, silk and rags.
sections.The Deepest Violence: The deepest colonial violence in language is not any single word — it is the invisibility. You can say 'judiação' or 'sold down the river' every day for decades without once thinking about the Inquisition or the slave trade. The language has digested its history so thoroughly that the history has become flavor, not substance. You taste it but you don't know what you're eating. This grammar is a nutritional label for the colonial diet. It doesn't tell you to stop eating. It tells you what's in the food.
sections.The Invitation: Read this grammar bilingually. For every Portuguese expression, find its English mirror. For every English idiom, find its Portuguese cousin. The mirrors reveal that no language is innocent — and no language is irredeemable. The colonial tongue can be heard, named, and gradually transformed. Not overnight. Not by decree. But word by word, song by song, lullaby by lullaby, as we learn to hear what we've been saying all along.
